steps.instruction: What weaknesses plagued the Soviet Union by the 1980s?
steps.work: The Soviet economy was stagnant — it couldn't produce enough consumer goods or food for its people. Military spending (keeping up with the U.S. arms race) consumed a huge share of the economy. The war in Afghanistan (1979-1989) drained resources and morale, much as Vietnam had for the U.S. Communist governments in Eastern Europe were unpopular and maintained control mainly through force.
steps.explanation: The Soviet system had deep structural problems. While it could build missiles and tanks, it couldn't provide its citizens with a decent standard of living — and people knew the West was doing better.

steps.instruction: Who were the main figures in ending the Cold War?
steps.work: Mikhail Gorbachev became Soviet leader in 1985 and introduced 'glasnost' (openness) and 'perestroika' (restructuring) — attempting to reform the Soviet system. President Ronald Reagan initially took a hard line against the Soviets ('evil empire'), increased military spending, and proposed the Strategic Defense Initiative (SDI). But Reagan and Gorbachev developed a working relationship and signed arms reduction treaties.
steps.explanation: It took leaders on both sides to end the Cold War. Gorbachev's willingness to reform and Reagan's willingness to negotiate (despite his tough rhetoric) were both essential.

steps.instruction: How did the Cold War actually end?
steps.work: In 1989, communist governments across Eastern Europe fell one after another — Poland, Hungary, Czechoslovakia, Romania. The Berlin Wall fell on November 9, 1989, as East Germans streamed into West Berlin. Germany reunified in 1990. In August 1991, a failed coup attempt in Moscow weakened Gorbachev. The Soviet Union officially dissolved on December 25, 1991, splitting into 15 independent nations.
steps.explanation: The Cold War ended not with a nuclear war but with the collapse of one side. The speed of the Soviet Union's fall surprised almost everyone — including U.S. intelligence agencies.

steps.instruction: Why does the end of the Cold War matter?
steps.work: The United States became the world's sole superpower. The threat of nuclear annihilation receded (though nuclear weapons still exist). Former Soviet states and Eastern European countries transitioned toward democracy and market economies — with mixed results. Americans debated what role the U.S. should play in a world without a clear rival.
steps.explanation: The end of the Cold War reshaped the entire world order. Questions about America's role as the sole superpower would define foreign policy debates for decades to come.

title: The Fall of the Berlin Wall

steps.instruction: Why was the Berlin Wall built, and what did it symbolize?
steps.work: After WWII, Germany was divided into democratic West Germany and communist East Germany. Berlin, located inside East Germany, was also divided. By 1961, so many East Germans were fleeing to the West that the communist government built a wall through the city — complete with guards, barbed wire, and orders to shoot anyone trying to cross. The Wall became the most powerful symbol of the Cold War division.
steps.explanation: The Berlin Wall was a physical manifestation of the Iron Curtain. It separated families, friends, and an entire city — a concrete reminder that the communist system had to imprison people to keep them from leaving.

steps.subgoalLabel: Describe the events leading to November 9, 1989
steps.instruction: What caused the Wall to come down?
steps.work: Gorbachev signaled he would not use Soviet troops to prop up Eastern European communist governments. Hungary opened its border with Austria, allowing East Germans to escape through there. Massive protests erupted in East German cities — hundreds of thousands of people demanding freedom. Under pressure, the East German government announced loosened travel rules on November 9, 1989.
steps.explanation: The Wall fell because of a combination of Soviet policy change, grassroots protest, and a cascade of events across Eastern Europe. People's desire for freedom proved stronger than concrete and barbed wire.

steps.subgoalLabel: Describe the night the Wall fell
steps.work: A government spokesman announced the new travel policy at a press conference and, when asked when it took effect, mistakenly said 'immediately.' Thousands of East Berliners rushed to the Wall. Overwhelmed border guards opened the gates. People climbed on top of the Wall, danced, embraced strangers, and began chipping away at it with hammers. Families separated for 28 years were reunited.
steps.explanation: The fall of the Berlin Wall was one of those rare moments when history happens live on television. It was partly the result of a bureaucratic miscommunication — but it captured the world's imagination as a triumph of human freedom.
